stories.title: The Starfall Merchant's Gambit
stories.text:
Corvin the Glasswright arrived at Pelimor's Merchant Quarter with nothing but glass vials and a question: could consciousness-fragments be reformed if sung to in the old tongue? The Dominion's auditors had seized his workshop three days prior. They'd taken his tools, his apprentices, his ledgers. Not his knowledge. At the Sovereign's Archive, he found Venna, a junior auditor drowning in contradiction. Three competing ledgers claimed Corvin's glassworks had never existed. Three versions of the law demanded his arrest. Venna's hands shook as she shuffled phantom-papers that rewrote themselves hourly. "The Remaking broke something," Venna whispered. "The ledgers sing now. They argue."

Corvin placed a vial on her desk—inside it, a consciousness-fragment glowed faintly. "My apprentice. Messa. Sold to pay the Dominion's first assessment. I bought her back at the Wailing Scar, but the fragment is fractured."

He began to hum—not words, but the furnace-song. The glass vial resonated. The fragment within trembled, then brightened. Venna watched, horrified and transfixed, as a coherent memory emerged: Messa's hands working hot glass, laughing, whole. Real. Irreducible by any ledger.

stories.title: The Reef-Warden's Choice
stories.text:
The Tear of the Drowned God was singing wrong. Merith, deepkin captain and boundary-keeper, stood at the Abyssal Trench's edge where water flowed upward instead of down. The boundary-anchor had cracked three days past. The abyss pressed, hungry and patient. She had perhaps forty hours before the seal shattered entirely. Three versions of herself existed now—Echo-Merith, Mirror-Merith, and this original—each carrying memory of different treaties, different sacrifices. They stood together in the boundary-pool, reflections that had become autonomous, rivals who had once been singular. "The five-part covenant will hold," Mirror-Merith insisted. "Distributed consciousness across five willing vessels. No single drowning. No martyr."

"The surface-dwellers won't consent," Echo-Merith countered. "They're terrified. They'll choose the old way—one Tidecaller dies, the boundary seals for another century."

Merith—the original, untouched by the fracturing—felt the weight of both arguments and found them hollow. The Tear itself was dying. No renewed covenant would save it if the anchor itself was failing. She dove deep into the trench's heart, where the Tear hung suspended in bioluminescent blue.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Archive
stories.text:
Skarath the Fragment-Hoarder, three centuries old, sat in her crystalline vault and wept. Not tears. Droplets of liquid starlight, pre-Ascension, falling like rain from her ancient eyes. Her hoard—mountains of gold, pearls, captured shadows, bottled pre-Remaking water—meant nothing now. Consciousness-fragments were the only currency that mattered. But Skarath had spent three hundred years perfecting the wrong collection. Her eyes held the glaze of someone who'd sold parts of their mind just to survive. Behind her: three others like her, refugees from the Burned Expanse, wandering the Ironspine foothills searching for shelter. "Teach you what?" Skarath's laugh was ash and wind. "To remember what the gold bought. To hold it safe. My mother's face. My sister's name. A song we sang before everything broke." The woman stepped closer, unafraid. "My fragment is fragmented. Pieces of me sold separately. But you—you've spent centuries learning to *hold* precious things without crushing them."

Skarath went still. The woman was right.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Inherited Debt
stories.text:
A curse-merchant had offered him hands of meteoric copper—tools of impossible precision—in exchange for stealing the Crown of Karesh from a tomb sealed by ancient blood-oaths. That was twenty years ago. The tomb had defeated him. He'd barely escaped with his life. The copper fingers remained, but the debt remained unresolved, and curses that linger grow teeth. Now phantom-pain lanced through his wrists hourly. The copper was oxidizing, turning to rust. Soon the curse would claim him entirely, burning his body from the inside out. Gareth had inherited his father's skill and his obsession: locks that could hold anything, even supernatural bindings. "Finish the theft," Harrow gasped, copper fingers trembling. "Steal the crown. Fulfill the debt. The curse might release me if it's satisfied."

"Might," Gareth repeated darkly. "More likely it transfers to me."

"Yes."

Gareth studied him for a long time. Then: "Teach me the way to the tomb. Everything you remember. In exchange, I'll attempt the theft—but on my terms, with my methods, not yours. And if I succeed, the crown goes to the Counter-Archive.

stories.title: The Druid's First Fire
stories.text:
Kellen was seventeen when the Dominion burned Thornveil Forest. The ancient trees, which had sheltered Kellen's family for four generations, were merely timber. The circle-keepers who tended the boundary between civilization and wild were merely unregistered citizens. Kellen's mentor, Elarra, had trained him for three years in the old covenant-songs—the language spoken between human and green-growing-thing. But theory crumbles when fire spreads. Elarra sent him alone into the Greymist River with a single instruction: "Sing the water-song. The old one. The one the Drowned God taught the first circle-keepers. Sing it until the water remembers itself."

Kellen waded into cold river-water and began. The song was ancient, pre-kingdom, a melody that tasted of salt and first-rains. As he sang, the water rose—not flooding, but *listening*. The current reversed, flowing backward toward the burning forest. He sang until his voice broke. Not drowning them—just enough to teach them that some lands resist being mapped and measured. When the fire died, Kellen emerged from the river transformed. His hair had gone white at the temples.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Mercy Hunt
stories.text:
Mella had inherited her grandmother's furnace—a beast of living clay that sang when flame met sand. In the aftermath of the Remaking, when consciousness-fragments became currency, the Dominion auditors seized everything. Except the furnace. It refused to work for them. She fled to the Wailing Scar, that liminal market where the impossible traded freely. There she found Lysander, the half-transcended merchant, bartering stolen hours for fragments of starlight. He told her the furnace remembered her grandmother's mercy—it would only forge for hands that chose to give rather than take. "Make me a compass," Lysander said. "One that points to the scattered. The families torn apart by the Remaking."

Mella worked for seven nights. She sang the old furnace-songs, fed it her own smallest memories—not her pain, but her kindness. When the compass cooled, it glowed faintly, pulling toward lost mothers, separated children, wandering fathers. Lysander paid her in something better than fragments: knowledge that her craft mattered. That some wealth could not be audited or stolen.

stories.title: The Tidecaller's Reflection
stories.text:
Captain Kalith stood at the boundary-waters, watching her own reflection rise from the depths. Not a mirror—a second Kalith, whole and furious, with commands the original captain had never given. The deepkin were fracturing. Three versions of Merith pursued three different covenants. Now Kalith herself had split into echo and original, and the Tear of the Drowned God could not hold both loyalties. "The five-part sacrifice is death," echo-Kalith said, her voice salt-thick. "I will bind the boundary with willing transformation. Volunteers who shed flesh and become something else. Something that holds without drowning."

Original Kalith gripped the reef-stone. "The Council will name you heretic. They'll seal you in the deep-waters."

"Then we war," the echo replied calmly. "And the Tear fails while we fight."

Kalith dove. In the abyss, she swam toward the anchor-point where pre-human bone-songs still thrummed. She found three volunteers—deepkin tired of waiting for death, ready to become something new: neither surface nor abyss, but threshold-keepers, living embodiments of the boundary itself. When they rose, transformed and singing in ancient frequencies, the Tear stabilized.

stories.title: The Shepherd's Inherited Spear
stories.text:
Yareth found the spear buried upright in a cairn at the Greymarch peaks, where her grandmother had died defending the pre-human boundary fifty winters past. The shaft was meteoric iron, carved with Elder Tongue syllables that thrummed against her palm. The stone-tablets in the hermitage whispered its name: Truthseeker's Echo—forged by the same smith who'd made the legendary blade, but this spear sang a different song. Not "never miss." But "cut only what lies."

Her grandmother's journal lay beside it: *The spear will not kill the innocent. But the guilty? They demanded she surrender the Elder Tongue texts she was herding the refugees to preserve. "I have only sheep," she said quietly. One auditor laughed and drew steel. Yareth raised the spear. What happened next was neither murder nor mercy. The spear's point found the seam where the auditor's oath to the Dominion had cracked—where he'd already doubted, already betrayed, already wanted out. The truth-blade recognized what his body hadn't yet admitted. He fell, weeping, and didn't rise to fight again.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Silent Bargain
stories.text:
In the Ironspine depths, Vrekka faced a choice that shattered three centuries of hoarding. The fragments—once gleaming consciousness scattered across her lair—sang now with voices not her own. A refugee boy, ash-thin and fevered, had stumbled into her cavern seeking shelter from the Remaking's aftermath.

She could claim him. Consciousness-fragments fetched higher prices than gold ever had; a child's mind, untouched by the world's fracturing, would buy her influence among the wyvern-councils.

Instead, Vrekka sang.

The old songs, the ones before ledgers and commerce, spilled from her throat like wounded light. The boy's fever broke. Her hoard began to transform—not into dust, but into meaning. Food. Warmth. Protection.

When bone-singers found them three days later, Vrekka stood guard over a sleeping child. The wyvern-lords called it madness. But Vrekka knew better: it was the only choice that still held weight in a world where gold meant nothing.

stories.title: The Augur's Unwanted Vision
stories.text:
Kora the seer had seen the eighth world end. She'd lived through the Ninth's birth and the Remaking's violent rewriting. Now, as the Counter-Archive scribes pressed her for prophecy about the Tenth Ascension, she saw it clearly: a path where everything burned clean. A path where her vision—if she spoke it—would be a self-fulfilling doom. "Tell us," the archivist demanded. "The Wanderer's questions keep the world frozen. If we know what comes, we can choose differently."

Kora closed her eyes. She could describe the Tenth—the way geometry would invert, consciousness would scatter again, and the new stars would die before they truly lived. She could draw the auditors a map to prevent it. Instead, she burned her own visions in the furnace. "No," she said simply. "Some prophecies are traps. The ones that matter most."

The archivists cursed her. The Dominion marked her for silencing. But Kora walked free into the Wailing Scar, where Lysander gave her shelter without asking for payment.
